speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This is an interview with Robert Ness on October the 31st, 1995, in which Mr. Ness will sing a variety of songs from several ghettos and camps. This interview is a supplementary interview to the original done on August the 24th, 1995, with Mr. Ness, and expands upon that one in a musical manner. The interview is being conducted in Mr. Ness's home at 4055 Lyons in Skokie, Illinois.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let us begin with the-- the Slonim ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the-- the song from the Slonim ghetto, [RUSSIAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please tell me where you learned the song.

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know precisely. I know I didn't learn it in the ghetto of Grodno. And I don't think I learned it in the ghetto of Vilna. So from this situation-- now, it's a possibility that I might have learned from this one of the fellows that I told you about in the Goldfields camp. But I think I must have learned it in Slonim. I am not sure, but it's kind of from the circumstances that I can combine of where, what-- I think it was from Slonim.

speaker: interviewer
text: The song--

speaker: interviewee
text: Keep in mind, Slonim, that was a Russian spoken city. But so was Grodno. Grodno, the intelligentsia, the Jewish intelligentsia especially, in Grodno, was speaking Russian even the-- even after the-- the-- even after the Polish independence in 1918.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this song is sung in-- you will sing it--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in Russian.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I know the song from beginning to end.

speaker: interviewer
text: It has a personal meaning for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it does.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is that meaning?

speaker: interviewee
text: The personal meaning was there is the passages where-- that a fellow goes to war and he says goodbye to his wife. And he-- he has a premonition that he's not going to return. And that he-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and-- and when I used to sing it for my father, to him it was when he said goodbye to his wife, when he was going outside of the ghetto of-- of Slonim. And my mother and-- and his sister remained. And then there was the killing of November 14. It's almost now 55, 54 years ago. November 14, 1941. So whenever my father asked me to sing it, he used to cry. I used to cry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because that's when your mother was killed in that massacre.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In the-- that's right. But in addition to that, it-- I could feel it was a good song, [RUSSIAN] one, because I used to sing it-- I told you-- for this Russian commander of the Kommandantur in east Germany where I was an interpreter--

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war. And he always used to ask me to sing it, too. And we used to drive in someplace and he used to ask me to sing [RUSSIAN]. So that is the story behind it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you sing the song?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll try to. But if I cry sometimes, you have to understand. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SINGING IN RUSSIAN] Now, this is a ballad, really, of-- I gave you the-- the English content of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [RUSSIAN] never came back from the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's right. He never came back. He-- she got the letter from the-- from the commander of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: His wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: --that-- his wife, that-- that's right. And that-- that he fall a death of the hero. In other words, he-- he fell in battle. But before dying, he remembered you, and he said goodbye to you again, like he did when he was saying goodbye before he went to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: To his wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said goodbye.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next we-- we have a song from the Grodno ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you-- this-- this song is going to be sung in Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as-- as far as the title, it is 'The Gates of the Ghetto.'

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say that most properly would be the right way description of it. It's a story of the-- of this Jewish policeman inside the ghetto who is dreaming about getting this job next to the gate so that he can earn something, some living, some-- a few dollar, a few marks on his side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because at the-- at the ghetto gate, there was a lot of activity.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That was the entrance. That was the main entrance. That is, you know, you want to smuggle something in-- food? The policeman knows, you know the policeman, and he tries to help you. So you have to pay him off, too. So that was the main action was at the entrance of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said marks because this-- this--

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time--

speaker: interviewer
text: --this is 1942--

speaker: interviewee
text: That is correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and Grodno is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was been incorporated--

speaker: interviewer
text: --incorporated--

speaker: interviewee
text: --in the Reich itself. It's part of Eastern Prussia. It was incorporated-- I think it was-- Allenberg, I think, was the capital in Eastern Prussia in which Grodno and Bialystok was part of the-- of the Third Reich. That is correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there is a-- there's a Kafkaesque quality.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there is no question about it. That why I-- how I remembered it, because it is a Kafkaesque quality. You have to imagine here is this policeman and he is watching-- what is he watching? He's on duty and guarding the cemetery. Now what is there to guard in the cemetery?

speaker: interviewer
text: The dead are there. The cemetery was part of the ghetto, in the ghetto. Ah, but there are young couples, you know? The-- the ghetto is crowded. So where do they go? They go in the cemetery. That's the only place where they can move around, hold on, or kiss or hug and whatnot. So in this respect, then he is there dreaming about getting the job at the gates and not being in the cemetery. So he's bemoaning his fate?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is correct. Yes. But he says, but their fate is a-- a rough one, too. I give him a-- a penalty, and-- but he said that fate is all that happens to me. And he dreams again about the-- the-- the job at the-- at the ghetto gates.

speaker: interviewer
text: You learned this song--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in Grodno. There's no question about it. It-- it's-- it's just only there. How or where I learned it? I don't know. Maybe from the fellows in the street. I liked-- I always liked to sing, and it was pretty, so I don't know. That's where I got it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember singing it in Grodno yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most probably. Most probably. Oh, yes. Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you sing it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. I-- I just sing just those two couplets that I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know the refrain and I know that one. OK. [SINGING IN POLISH] And there is also a passage in some place-- I don't know where it fits in-- where he says [SPEAKING POLISH] But we don't need much for-- for life. [SPEAKING POLISH] Five mark and a loaf of bread. So-- but it's-- it must be in the second couplet, but it's all I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks. From Grodno, you escaped with your-- your sister and her boyfriend.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I-- First was-- my father and I went first, and then they came later. But yes, we escaped to Vilna. And Tadek was the one-- as a matter of fact, I talked to him. He's in-- I talked to him a few days ago. I asked him that he should send some photographs because I wanted to do something to show him at the time as-- as he looked, and so on. He was honored in Jerusalem as one of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: A righteous Gentile.

speaker: interviewee
text: --righteous-- yes. Yes. As one of the righteous of the world. And he was here in Chicago, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And so he brought us over there on the train. But that is-- I gave you that story before. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the Vilna ghetto, there was a-- a theater.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh! That is-- to me, it was-- I never saw such a thing. Never heard of such a thing. A ghetto. A theater in a ghetto! I mean, it was unheard of. And here it was, by gosh. And performances. And-- well, I-- it-- it was really something. And I used to run there. I used to go there as often as twice a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the theater, you heard a number of songs--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that you remember to this day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I remember distinctly. One of them I mentioned to you was about this Yoshke. That was-- I might have sang it last time. I don't think so. I don't remember. But, anyhow, Yoshke is a fellow that goes to the army-- in the Czarist army-- and he's dressed up on the stage as the one with the-- with the boots and the pants over, and the-- and the shirt out. You know the Russian type of military shirt that goes-- what they call [SPEAKING RUSSIAN]. And-- and a girl is next to him in the-- in the-- dressed up like a 19th century kind of farm girl with the babushka and with her pantaloon, with her underwear showing, the long pant-- underpants.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she comes to the train to say goodbye to Yoshke, who is going to the army. And there-- it's a combination of all kind of songs in between thrown in. And it-- it is-- anyhow, and she tells him, you know, why do you-- why do you have to be a soldier? You shouldn't be a soldier. And he says, what do you think, I'm such a rag? And says, why don't we get married and get it done with? And she says, my father was a rabbi. He says, well, if you're talking about [YIDDISH], meaning if you are talking about-- what would you call it? Connections.

speaker: interviewee
text: Connections. He says, you know, my-- so then he-- she comes up. Your father was a-- a nobody, kind of smearing oil on the-- on the wheels, and your mother was stealing fish in the market. And your sister was living with a Cossack. And finally, the train comes and the-- it whistles, and it goes away. And that's it. But it was a hilarious type of a performance on the stage. I loved it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you mind singing that song?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. I know that song all the way from the beginning to end. [SINGING IN YIDDISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: [TRAIN WHISTLE SOUND] [LAUGHTER] Very good. Very good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. I always liked that song. I liked it so much, I-- and I did sing it in the camps. And I sang it after the war. And I used to entertain some of the friends that used to come and knew Yiddish, so I used to sing it for them. Yes. I like that song. It was a-- because it's partly kind of a potpourri of a number of songs like-- that are in there, you know. Folk songs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember in Vilna in the theater when you heard it, what the response of the audience was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, the crowd was clapping, you know. They were enjoying it. Oh, yes. Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it-- did you-- do you remember singing it to your father, or to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. Sure. I liked that song. I knew it by heart. I knew the whole song from the beginning to end, and I understood all of the connections and the cultural annotations of the speak, and the-- the-- the finer points on it. So, yes. Yes. I enjoyed it very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the-- even though now the end of the-- at the end of the song, Yoshke goes away--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yoshke goes away, and that's it. That's the end of it. And now she--

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it says-- the song says-- [SINGING IN YIDDISH] I went to the train to accompany-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] which is her friend, Yoshke, to-- and I stayed at the station. So in other words, she's accompanying here the soldier who goes to the army. He's being drafted. And now, he's-- keep in mind, we are talking now in the Czarist army before the First World War.

speaker: interviewer
text: So even though the song ends with a parting--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the parting. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --it's not a happy ending, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: What's that?

speaker: interviewer
text: It's not a happy ending, but still it's a happy song.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It's a happy song.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a humorous song.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's-- it is-- they kind of know they are parting, and the train leaves and that's it. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's it. There were other songs--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that you heard in the Vilna theater. And one of them was 'The Same Streets and Street Cars.'

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but in-- for that one to sing, I would have to refresh my recollection. And I did it. I got it only yesterday from a copy of-- some of it, I remember by heart. I think you have the English so I don't have to translate that part of it, no? It-- it's just the 'Does it Have to be this Way.' This is the one you're talking about, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shall I give you the English part of it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Just a-- a little synopsis, and in general.

speaker: interviewee
text: It talks about the same street and the same trolleys, the children-- or the same children are calling out the newspapers. And-- and here, this boy is closed in in the ghetto. Cannot go. [GERMAN]. It's not allowed.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's-- why does it have to be this way? Who constructed the world like that, that for one it's all allowed and the other one should-- says should starve. And it goes on along those lines. It's-- it's-- I-- it's-- that's basically what it is.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, you know, there's one that his-- his fate is such that he is just dirt and-- not dirt, but he's really being persecuted. And-- and the other side is as free, moves around as if nothing happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this song was sung--

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that song distinctly. Yes. First of all, I was the age of 14. So it-- 14.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1943. That's right. You know, I was in the ghetto of Vilna only from March through September. So the whole thing was six months that I was in the ghetto Vilna. Not even six months. It was probably five months, because on September 3 I was caught, 1943, and taken to Estonia. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember that song. I liked it. It has kind of a-- it's not only the local-- the local flavor, it has this general type, you know, more philosophical, universal. Who's constructed such a world? Who permitted these kind of things to happen? In-- in that sense, it's a little bit beyond the local scene in general. It's-- it's that-- kind of like in the-- like the story from Grodno. This is specific, local policeman watching a cemetery, dreaming about the gate. There is no big philosophizing on the subject, you know? And here, this little boy, he sees the injustice that goes on and the slaughter, and-- and he just says why, why, why? The question is why. And that song, the title is [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. 'Does it Has to be Like That?' Would you sing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. I think maybe I would do better if I read-- if I sing it straight from the Yiddish part of that part, OK? [SINGING IN YIDDISH] That's basically it. [DRINKS] It did have a touch, in-- that song always did.

speaker: interviewer
text: That-- that eternal question of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No question about it. No question about it. Because the local stuff-- OK, ghetto, you know. But here is this philosophical-- the-- the-- the-- the question of is there essence of it. Why-- why should it be like that? Who did it like that? Who constructed such a thing? [CRYING] And it's not merely just a question of gooder life, bad, good life, better life. I mean, it's life and death.

speaker: interviewer
text: [CRYING] The whys of injustice.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. The why. Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a-- another song that-- that you heard in the theater. A-- the song [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewee
text: That is-- yes, it's quite a song. It's-- I don't remember fully the word, but I know some of this part. I just got it, too. It is the song that's the steps. The song goes, one, two, three, one, two, three-- the marching. And it talks about, you know, it's good when you know where you're going, for what and for why. Because here the people are marching, they're marching to the graves.

speaker: interviewer
text: That--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the question is asked, where is your-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is your wife? Where is our child? Where is your whole-- with the whole-- the whole-- all of your-- all of your crowd? And it ends as [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Meaning they're marching to death. So the 1, 2, 3, 1, 2, 3 is the-- is the death march.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's really the death march. It's exactly the point. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the death march was to Ponary.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Ponary. Yeah, sure. Ponary. That's where most of the Jews of Vilna were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was sung in the-- this was sung in the theater--

speaker: interviewee
text: See, I don't-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't remember that strongly. It might have been. It might have been. It's-- I just-- you know, the reason I am saying it might have been-- I cannot-- cannot-- after-- you know, afterwards when you start thinking that to permit something like that in the German occupation, to sing something like that in the ghetto, it-- you would kind of think like it's crazy that every-- that-- that they would permit something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And yet, as I told you, I saw there once a performance showing [HEBREW] with a-- with a shield, with a [YIDDISH] David-- the yellow-- and a sword in his hand fighting the Romans. I mean-- which-- and obviously the allegory is to the-- to the Germans-- the Romans. And here that this great warrior, [HEBREW] from biblical times. You know? So I wouldn't be surprised that maybe that went through and nobody paid attention, and so on. But I know I learned it in-- in-- in-- in-- in Vilna, and I sang it. And I sang it in the camps later, too.
